steps.title: Recognize the exponential growth pattern
steps.content: The scatterplot shows data points that accelerate upward: points near $x = 0$–$3$ cluster in the lower region (around 100–500), while points at $x = 5$–$7$ spread into the thousands (2,200–5,000). The gap between consecutive points widens as $x$ increases. This accelerating growth is characteristic of exponential behavior, not linear. A straight line cannot capture this pattern of increasing acceleration.

steps.title: Eliminate linear models
steps.content: Choices A and B both propose straight lines as the model. For choice A, the line passes through roughly (0, 0) and (6, 4,290), leaving 7 points badly below the line and only 1 above — a severely unbalanced distribution. Choice B has a downward-sloping line, which contradicts the clearly upward trend in the data. Neither linear model can fit exponential data appropriately. Exponential data requires a curved model.

steps.title: Compare the two exponential curves
steps.content: Both choices C and D propose exponential curves, but they differ in how well the points scatter around them. Choice C has all 8 data points below the curve, meaning the curve is positioned too high and overshoots the data systematically. Choice D has 2 points touching the curve, 3 points above it, and 3 points below it — a balanced distribution. A model of best fit should have residuals (deviations of points from the curve) balanced on both sides. This balanced placement indicates the curve in choice D accurately captures the underlying exponential relationship. That matches choice D — D is correct.
